sections.title: 1. Indefinite Articles (Indefiniter Artikel)
sections.description: Use these when introducing something for the first time or when referring to a non-specific item (equivalent to "a" or "an" in English).

rules.rule: Nominative: Used for the subject of the sentence or after the verb sein (to be).
rules.example: Das ist ein Stuhl. (That is a chair.)

rules.rule: Accusative: Used when the noun is the direct object of an action. Note: Only the masculine article changes from ein to einen.
rules.example: Ich möchte einen Kaffee. (I would like a coffee.)

sections.title: 2. Definite Articles (Definiter Artikel)
sections.description: Use these when referring to a specific item already mentioned or known to the listener (equivalent to "the" in English).

rules.rule: Specific Identification: Once you have introduced "a chair" (ein Stuhl), it becomes "the chair" (der Stuhl).
rules.example: Das ist ein Laptop. Der Laptop ist gut. (That is a laptop. The laptop is good.)

rules.rule: Accusative Change: Just like indefinite articles, only the masculine changes from der to den.
rules.example: Ich finde den Rock schön. (I find the skirt beautiful.)

sections.title: 3. Zero Article (Kein Artikel)
sections.description: In certain German sentences, you do not use an article at all.

key_rules.description: Concepts you cannot count (Time, Luck, Money, Hunger).
examples: Ich habe Zeit. (I have time.) — Not "a time"

key_rules.description: When saying what your job is.
examples: Ich bin Pilotin. (I am a pilot.) — In English we say "a pilot", but in German, we omit the article.

key_rules.description: When speaking about more than one item in a general sense.

sections.title: 4. Practical Application (From Exercises)
sections.description: How to transition between articles in real usage.

transitioning.title: Transitioning from Indefinite to Definite
transitioning.description: When describing objects, you usually follow this pattern:

accusative_questions.title: The Accusative Case in Questions
accusative_questions.description: When asking "Do you have...?" (Hast du...?), the object you are asking about is in the Accusative Case.

examples.note: Did you notice the -en ending? This is because Kuli is masculine and the direct object.

sections.title: 5. Narrative Usage (The "Märchen" Example)
sections.description: In storytelling, the article changes as the story progresses.

example.introduction: Es war einmal ein König. (Once upon a time there was a king - first introduction)
example.continuation: Der König war sehr reich. (The king was very rich - now we know which king we are talking about)

content.grammar_tip: Always learn the gender (der/die/das) with every new noun. If you don't know the gender, you cannot choose the correct article for Nominative or Accusative cases!
